titleStmt.title: Digital Camerarius – Tracing the Classical origins of Pre-Linnean Science

author.affiliation: Furman University, United States of America
AIT Austrian Institute of Technology GmbH
Furman University, United States of America

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

body.p:
This poster presents the Digital Camerarius, a digital edition of the Symbola et Emblemata by Joachim Camerarius. The goal of the project is to provide a machine-readable transcription enriched with structural and semantic markup, and to facilitate multimodal exploration with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). The project is a work in progress managed by Classics undergraduates at Furman University (
https://furman-editions-in-progress.github.io/camerarius/ ) with the collaboration of AIT Austrian Institute of Technology.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Joachim Camerarius the Younger (1534-1598) was a German botanist, zoologist, and humanist. The Symbola et Emblemata, one of the most influential emblem books of Early Modern Europe, was published in several editions between 1595 and 1668, with the last edition divided in four centuriae, each focusing on plants, animals, reptiles and birds, or aquatic animals. The work is a collection of emblems with one-page descriptions of each exemplar, where empirical information is mixed with citations and quotations from ancient sources. As such, the Symbola document an intellectual environment half-way between a moral-religious Weltanschauung entangled with Classical knowledge, and the rationalism of the Enlightenment.

div.head: Workflow
div.p: Figure 1. A standard emblem page in Transkribus.

The workflow includes automatic layout and text recognition, which are manually revised by undergraduates at Furman University. Text and Layout recognition are performed using the Noscemus GM 6 model provided by Transkribus (Fröstl et al. 2023). The resulting XML/TEI transcription is enriched with custom markup for places, languages, and citations. Named Entities are matched against WikiData wherever possible. Classical sources, when identifiable, are cross-referenced to passages or works in the Scaife Digital Library through CTS URNs.

Multimodal search will be enhanced by a RAG process, which generates a response to the user query in combination with the retrieval mechanism (Li et al. 2024). Both text and images can be queried for contents with natural language prompts, analogously to models already established in projects analyzing early scientific depictions, e.g. Emblematica Online (
http://emblematica.library.illinois.edu/ ), and ONiT explorer (
https://travelogues.app/ , Vignoli et al. 2024). The prompt is passed to the retriever, which provides the most relevant sources from the previously created Marqo index. Subsequently, the RAG generates a suitable answer to the user question based on the most relevant data provided by the retriever. In addition to summarising the content found, the AI will generate a transparent collection of direct, further links to the original resources used as a basis for creating the answer.

div.head: Conclusion and future work
div.p: The project is the result of several summer research fellowships. Currently, the Index Locorum of recognizable citations includes 169 corrected entries, and approximately 30 fully reviewed entries with the corresponding emblems and captions. While in the previous years the transcription work had been almost entirely manual, switching to an automatic recognition model will enable us to work much faster than in previous years.

One of the most important challenges is the structured markup of text reuses, such as quotations and citations from other authors: these are sometimes vague, with omitted information, translated in a different language, quoted from anthologies, or abbreviated in non-standard ways. However, they are especially important to understand the background of Camerarius’ knowledge and the dynamics of text reuse in the Symbola.
Figure 3. Current tagset used for quotations in Camerarius.

This work will enable the production of the first computationally manipulable version of Camerarius’ work, and it will make use of state-of-the-art AI-technology to support the extraction of multimodal information, enabling the exploration of his library, the analysis of the interplay between text and image, and the investigation of the continuous interchange between ancient knowledge and early modern science.

bibl.hi: Fröstl, Michael / Zathammer, Stefan / Lang, Sarah
(2023): "Zur Transkription von Alchemica mithilfe der Transkribus-Software: Zu Handschriften, Drucken und dem NOSCEMUS GM 6 Modell", in: Lang, Sarah (ed.):
. Alchemical Laboratories. Graz: Graz University Library Publishing 363–378. DOI: 10.25364/978390337404119.
Li, Xiaoxi / Jin, Jiajie / Zhou, Yujia / Zhang, Yuyao / Zhang, Peitian / Zhu, Yutao / Dou, Zhicheng
From Matching to Generation: A Survey on Generative Information Retrieval
LLM-TAKE: Theme Aware Keyword Extraction Using Large Language Models
(2024): "Survey of vector database management systems", in:
(2024): "Revolution or Evolution? AI-Driven Retrieval of Nature Representations in Historical Prints", in:
